revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Shimazu|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Shimazu Tadahisa.jpg|thumb|right]]
{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the [[Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] [[samurai]] clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[Osaka]]. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''[[jitō]]'' (land steward) of the [[Shimazu Estate]] by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of [[Hiki Yoshikazu]].
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''[[Shugo]]'' of [[Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.
{{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shimazu, Tadahisa}} [[Category:1227 deaths]] [[Category:Daimyo]] [[Category:Shimazu clan]] [[Category:12th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:13th-century Japanese people]]

{{family name hatnote|Shimazu|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Shimazu Tadahisa.jpg|thumb|right]]
{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the [[Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] [[samurai]] clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[Osaka]]. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''[[jitō]]'' (land steward) of the [[Shimazu Estate]] by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of [[Hiki Yoshikazu]].
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''[[Shugo]]'' of [[Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.

{{family name hatnote|Shimazu|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Shimazu Tadahisa.jpg|thumb|right]]
{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the [[Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] [[samurai]] clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[Osaka]]. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''[[jitō]]'' (land steward) of the [[Shimazu Estate]] by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of [[Hiki Yoshikazu]].
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''[[Shugo]]'' of [[Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.

{{family name hatnote|Shimazu|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Shimazu Tadahisa.jpg|thumb|right]]
{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the [[Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] [[samurai]] clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[Osaka]]. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''[[jitō]]'' (land steward) of the [[Shimazu Estate]] by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of [[Hiki Yoshikazu]].
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''[[Shugo]]'' of [[Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.
{{Authority control}}

{{Infobox person|image=File:Shimazu Tadahisa.jpg|birth_date=1200; {{birth date|1180|1|29}}|death_date={{death date|1227|8|1}}|birth_place={{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|bushi|qid=Q1975935}}; {{ill|samurai|qid=Q38142}}|title={{ill|daimyo|qid=Q61982}}|spouse={{ill|Q111366580|qid=Q111366580}}|children={{ill|Shimazu Tadatoki|qid=Q11162886}}|father={{ill|Koremune no Hirokoto|qid=Q11492951}}; {{ill|Koremune no Tadayasu|qid=Q11073324}}|mother={{ill|Gishūmon-in no Tango|qid=Q3107786}}; {{ill|Q111366618|qid=Q111366618}}; {{ill|Tango no Naishi|qid=Q11368515}}|relatives={{ill|Tsutsumi Tadasue|qid=Q11163129}}|family={{ill|Shimazu clan|qid=Q1061406}}|native_name=島津忠久|given_name={{ill|Tadahisa|qid=Q122387586}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3482119]] Wikidata name: Shimazu Tadahisa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Shimazu|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Shimazu Tadahisa.jpg|thumb|right]]
{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''[[:en:Jitō|Jitō]]'' (land steward) of the [[:en:Shimazu Estate|Shimazu Estate]] by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of [[:en:Hiki Yoshikazu|Hiki Yoshikazu]].
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[:en:Shinano province|Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[:en:Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shimazu Tadahisa]][[en:Shimazu Tadahisa]][[fa:شیمازو تاداهیسا]][[fr:Shimazu Tadahisa]][[it:Shimazu Tadahisa]][[ja:島津忠久]][[ko:시마즈 다다히사 (헤이안 시대)]][[pt:Shimazu Tadahisa]][[zh-classical:惟宗忠久]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Shimazu Tadahisa.jpg|birth_date=1200; {{birth date|1180|1|29}}|death_date={{death date|1227|8|1}}|birth_place={{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|bushi|WD=Q1975935}}; {{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142}}|title={{ill|daimyo|WD=Q61982}}|spouse={{ill|Q111366580|WD=Q111366580}}|children={{ill|Shimazu Tadatoki|WD=Q11162886}}|father={{ill|Koremune no Hirokoto|WD=Q11492951}}; {{ill|Koremune no Tadayasu|WD=Q11073324}}|mother={{ill|Gishūmon-in no Tango|WD=Q3107786}}; {{ill|Q111366618|WD=Q111366618}}; {{ill|Tango no Naishi|WD=Q11368515}}|relatives={{ill|Tsutsumi Tadasue|WD=Q11163129}}|family={{ill|Shimazu clan|WD=Q1061406}}|native_name=島津忠久|given_name={{ill|Tadahisa|WD=Q122387586}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3482119]] Wikidata name: Shimazu Tadahisa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Shimazu|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Shimazu Tadahisa.jpg|thumb|right]]
{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''[[:en:Jitō|Jitō]]'' (land steward) of the [[:en:Shimazu Estate|Shimazu Estate]] by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of [[:en:Hiki Yoshikazu|Hiki Yoshikazu]].
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[:en:Shinano province|Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[:en:Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.

He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.

{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''[[:en:Jitō|Jitō]]'' (land steward) of the [[:en:Shimazu Estate|Shimazu Estate]] by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of [[:en:Hiki Yoshikazu|Hiki Yoshikazu]].
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[:en:Shinano province|Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[:en:Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.

He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu]] clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.

{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|WD=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu}} {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}}'' (land steward) of the {{ill|Shimazu Estate|en|Shimazu Estate|WD=Q11476819|lt=Shimazu Estate}} by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of {{ill|Hiki Yoshikazu|en|Hiki Yoshikazu|WD=Q5760562|lt=Hiki Yoshikazu}}.
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[:en:Shinano province|Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' of [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[:en:Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|WD=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu}} clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.
{{Authority control}}

According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}}'' (land steward) of the {{ill|Shimazu Estate|en|Shimazu Estate|WD=Q11476819|lt=Shimazu Estate}} by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of {{ill|Hiki Yoshikazu|en|Hiki Yoshikazu|WD=Q5760562|lt=Hiki Yoshikazu}}.
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[:en:Shinano province|Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' of [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[:en:Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|WD=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu}} clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.
{{Authority control}}

According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō|1=Jitō}}'' (land steward) of the {{ill|Shimazu Estate|en|Shimazu Estate|WD=Q11476819|lt=Shimazu Estate|1=Shimazu Estate}} by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of {{ill|Hiki Yoshikazu|en|Hiki Yoshikazu|WD=Q5760562|lt=Hiki Yoshikazu|1=Hiki Yoshikazu}}.
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[:en:Shinano province|Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' of [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[:en:Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|WD=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu|1=Shimazu clan}} clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.
{{Authority control}}

{{nihongo|'''Shimazu Tadahisa'''|島津 忠久||1179-1227}} was the founder of the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|WD=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu|1=Shimazu clan}} {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} clan.
According to a record of his life, he was reportedly born in [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. He was initially {{nihongo|''Koremune no Tadahisa''|惟宗忠久}} but after being given the position of ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō|1=Jitō}}'' (land steward) of the {{ill|Shimazu Estate|en|Shimazu Estate|WD=Q11476819|lt=Shimazu Estate|1=Shimazu Estate}} by [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], he took the name of Shimazu.
Tadahisa was a son of the Shōgun [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] (1147–1199) by the sister of {{ill|Hiki Yoshikazu|en|Hiki Yoshikazu|WD=Q5760562|lt=Hiki Yoshikazu|1=Hiki Yoshikazu}}.
He then received the domain of Shioda ([[:en:Shinano province|Shinano province]]) in 1186 and was then named ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' of [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma province]]. He sent Honda Sadachika to take possession of the province in his name and accompanied Yoritomo in his expedition to [[:en:Mutsu province|Mutsu]] in 1189. He went to Satsuma in 1196, subdued Hyūga and [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi province]]s, and built a castle in the domain of Shimazu (Hyūga) which name he also adopted. He died in 1227 and was buried in Kamakura, near his father's tomb. His descendants ran the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|WD=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu|1=Shimazu clan}} clan (named after him) for hundreds of years.
==References== *Sansom, George (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Turnbull, Stephen (1998). ''The Samurai Sourcebook.'' London: Cassell & Co.
